data.title: GSST: Goldman Sachs' Short-Term Bond Fund
data.description:
Thesis

Coming from Goldman Sachs Asset Management, the Goldman Sachs Access Ultra Short Bond ETF (BATS:GSST) represents the fixed income offering in the short duration bond space from GS. The fund seeks to provide current income with capital preservation, via a portfolio of investment grade bonds and MBS securities. The ETF is fairly new, having IPO-ed only in 2019.

While there are many choices in the short bond space, large asset management platforms tend to have the most compelling offerings, relying on their unparalleled platform research capabilities and human capital. To that end, we will show you how GSST has outperformed versus a cohort of peers.

In today's article we are going to have a closer look at GSST, its portfolio and structuring, and benchmark this short-term bond fund versus its peers.

Fund composition

Given its mandate and objectives, the name focuses on short dated investment grade bonds, MBS securities and Treasuries:

IG bonds represent 37% of the fund, followed by MBS securities at 24% and ABS bonds at 24%. From a ratings perspective, most names are AAA or AA:

The fund splits the government backed treasury and Agency securities in a separate sector, but we can consider them AAA for the purpose of a ratings matrix. The riskiest IG bonds, namely BBB rated ones, represent only 7.5% of the holdings in this fund. Please note, the above parsing denotes a fund which runs very little credit risk, with the name being mostly affected by rates in the short term. Its low duration also allows for a low probability of default for the residual corporate risk.

The name comes with a 0.56 years duration and a 4.94% 30-day SEC yield. Additional analytics are as follows:

  • AUM: $0.6 billion
  • Sharpe Ratio: -0.34 (3Y)
  • Std. Deviation: 1.1 (3Y)
  • Yield: 4.94% (30-day SEC)
  • Leverage Ratio: 0%
  • Composition: Treasuries, MBS bonds, IG bonds
  • Duration: 0.56 years
  • Expense Ratio: 0.2%

How does it stack up against the competition?

The fund falls in a cohort that includes many other competitors from the largest asset management platforms around. In fact, the Seeking Alpha platform has a dedicated tab for all ultra-short bond funds that an investor can consult here.

The main aspects to consider when looking at GSST and its competitors are the ones regarding yields and duration considerations. In a monetary loosening environment (today's environment), an investor wants to have as much duration as possible for a well-built short-term fund.

When looking on a 1-year period, GSST has done very well against its peers:

The fund has beaten on a 1-year look-back the PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Active ETF (MINT), the JPMorgan Ultra-Short Income ETF (JPST) and the SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL). In fact, GSST's active management has allowed for a rough 50 bps outperformance versus its closest peer, a figure which is very significant when considering the low yielding nature of the asset class.

BIL is the lowest performer since it only contains risk-free treasuries, all while the rest of the names are overweight corporate bonds and MBS securities. While small, the incremental credit risk taken by GSST does provide for an additional yield enhancement.

What is the forward for GSST?

The fund is a short duration bond one, thus fully dependent on short-term yields. With the Fed having started its easing campaign, the front end of the curve has moved lower, and is expected to continue to decrease. All short dated bond fund will be affected, but with a certain lag. Ultimately, GSST provides for a spread over short-term treasury yields; thus the yield equation is short-term treasury yields plus credit spread. As short-term yields move lower, GSST's 30-day SEC yield will decrease as well.

We believe GSST will continue to deliver outsized value for its cohort via active management and security selection, but do not expect all-in yields to remain constant or increase here. It is a well-built fund, but a fund that cannot escape the overall macro theme of lower front-end rates. We like the fund's granular build with a solid ratings band composition, with GSST representing an attractive choice in the space.

While the next 12 months will see very aggressive Fed cuts as per the SOFR forward curve, the market does see a bottoming out in rates at a 3.2% level:

Courtesy of Chatham, we can see above the 1-month forward SOFR curve, a curve which bottoms out at 3.2% currently after January 2026. This simply means that the forward market sees long-term neutral rate levels at above 3%. The forward market is actively traded, and these levels will change.

The current picture, however, allows us to imply a long term 30-day SEC yield for GSST of roughly 4% as things stand, i.e. another 100 bps drop from the current level. Kindly keep in mind that GSST has a credit component, thus will always yield more than Fed Funds or SOFR for that matter, as we have seen from its performance graph.

Conclusion

GSST is a fixed income exchange-traded fund. The name is the Goldman offering in the short-term bond space, and comes with the research capabilities provided by the GS platform. The ETF has outperformed a cohort of peers in the past year, and has favorable analytics. Despite its pedigree, the fund is an expression of short-term rates with a credit spread on top, and will offer a decreasing yield as Fed Funds move lower. We like GSST and its build, and believe the ETF represents a robust choice in the short-term fund space. Investors, however, should be fully comfortable with a name that will continue to see its yield decrease to a level which we predict to be 4% based on the market priced new neutral rate.

data.title: uniQure's Yellow Flags: Early-Stage Gene Therapies Amid Restructuring
data.description:
uniQure N.V. (NASDAQ:QURE) leverages its adeno-associated virus [AAV] technology and proprietary platforms. In particular, it relies on its Smart AAV, miQURE, LinQURE, and GoQURE IP to develop one-time gene therapies for liver-directed and central nervous system [CNS] diseases. This way, QURE can develop gene therapies that silence and replace genes, potentially targeting several diseases. Its pipeline includes one FDA-approved treatment for hemophilia B and investigational drugs for Huntington's disease, Fabry disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis [ALS], temporal lobe epilepsy, and autosomal dominant Alzheimer's disease. Yet, at this point, I feel QURE's ongoing restructuring adds another risk layer. It's possible that this could pay off in the long term, but for now, I believe it's prudent to wait and see how it evolves with its next quarterly report. Thus, I rate QURE a "Hold" for now, but it is worth adding to your watchlist.

Overview: Rare Diseases And Gene Therapy

uniQure N.V. is a biotechnology company founded in 1998 and headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands. The company aims to generate one-time treatments delivering therapeutic cures using adeno-associated virus [AAV] technology. It develops gene therapies for liver-directed and central nervous system [CNS] illnesses. Notably, QURE has been refining AAV technology for over two decades to provide targeted treatments to specific tissues.

QURE's delivery platform, Smart AAV, uses capsids to supply the therapeutic genes. Other platforms include miQURE, LinQURE, and GoQURE. It's worth highlighting that miQURE is a proprietary system for gene silencing that degrades genes that cause disease without impacting other cellular processes. Similarly, GoQURE incorporates gene silencing with gene replacement within one AAV vector. This approach removes the mutated gene's toxic products while repairing function by replacing the faulty gene with a healthy one.

Lastly, LinQURE can supply several microRNAs within an AAV vector for concurrent gene silencing of multiple genes or signaling pathways. After all, while microRNAs are small, non-coding RNAs, they still help regulate gene expression. This is why QURE's LinQURE technology is also helpful for addressing complex conditions related to various genetic factors or biological pathways.

The company's portfolio includes HEMGENIX [AMT-061], an FDA-approved hemophilia B product commercially available in the US, EU, UK, and Canada. This treatment involves a one-time delivery of a functional copy of the gene that produces factor IX, a protein necessary for blood clotting. QURE has a partnership with CSL Behring, a segment of CSL Limited (OTCQX:CSLLY), to commercialize this product.

Currently, QURE's pipeline contains the drug candidate AMT-130 in Phase 1/2 for Huntington's disease [HD]. HD is a condition caused by mutations in the huntingtin gene that generate an abnormal huntingtin protein that causes neurodegeneration. Thus, AMT-130 delivers gene-silence therapeutics to reduce the production of toxic huntingtin proteins and slow or halt the disease's progression. Similarly, AMT-162 is another drug candidate in Phase 1/2 trials targeting neurodegenerative processes in Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis [ALS]. In this case, AMT-162 focuses on mutations related to the SOD1 gene [SOD1-ALS]. Another candidate called AMT-260 is an investigational therapy in early Phase 1/2 trials for temporal lobe epilepsy that affects memory and emotion.

Furthermore, AMT-191 is in early Phase ½ as a potential cure for Fabry disease. Here, AMT-191's mechanism of action works by delivering genetic material that produces the enzyme alpha-galactosidase A [alpha-GAL]. The idea is that this approach compensates for alpha-GAL insufficiency in Fabry disease, which theoretically addresses its root cause. Lastly, QURE has two preclinical research programs, AMT-161 and AMT-240. AMT-161 is indicated for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis linked to the c9orf72 gene [c9orf72-ALS], while AMT-240 targets autosomal dominant Alzheimer's disease.

More recently, in August 2024, QURE began dosing patients with AMT-191 for Fabry disease. Since this condition limits the body's ability to break down specific fats, it can lead to life-threatening kidney, heart, and CNS complications. Shortly after, in September 2024, the FDA granted AMT-191 Orphan Drug Designation. In my view, this shows that regulators see its potential for curing this rare disorder. However, more importantly, I think it also somewhat validates QURE's overall platform.

Interestingly, just this month, QURE initiated its EPISOD1 trial for AMT-162 to treat SOD1-ALS. As previously noted, this is a Phase 1/2 trial that will test its efficacy for dealing with neurodegenerative processes, muscle weakness, and paralysis. Since SOD1-ALS leads to a much shorter life expectancy compared to other versions of ALS, it represents a potential niche with unmet needs that QURE could eventually address. The study is open-label, meaning participants and researchers know the administered therapy. It's also worth highlighting that EPISOD1 will use biomarkers that should let QURE create proof-of-concept results rapidly, potentially expediting its approval process. Yet, despite its promising prospects, it's still a very early-stage research program.

Limited Upside: Valuation Analysis

From a valuation perspective, QURE trades at a $305.8 million market cap, making it a microcap in its sector. Its balance sheet holds $287.9 million in cash and equivalents and $236.6 million in short-term investments. This amounts to $524.5 million in short-term available liquidity against $102.5 million of financial debt. Its book value stands at $97.1 million, indicating a P/B of 3.2. For comparison, its sector's median P/B is 2.4, suggesting that QURE is somewhat overvalued relative to its peers.

Moreover, I estimate its latest quarterly cash burn was $33.3 million by adding its CFOs and Net CAPEX. QURE's Q2 2024 had negative $32.7 million in cash flows from operations and spent $0.6 million in CAPEX. Thus, that implies a yearly cash burn run rate of about $133.2 million. This suggests a cash runway of approximately 3.9 years, which seems enough for its near-term operations.

However, I do think there's a moderate likelihood of another raise in the next couple of years because even AMT-130 is still years away from any regulatory approval. Likewise, AMT-260 is in its very early stages. So until QURE secures any other FDA approvals, its only revenue-generating product will remain AMT-061 (Hemgenix) for Hemophilia B. According to Seeking Alpha's dashboard on QURE, the company is projected to generate $65.1 million in royalties by 2025. Yet, this rare disease genetic therapy doesn't seem enough to justify considerable upside potential for the stock.

This implies that QURE has a forward P/S of 4.7. For comparison, its sector's median forward P/S is 3.8, so again, QURE seems expensive compared to its industry. Therefore, I think that on balance, the stock's upside seems limited at this juncture. Likewise, I don't see any major flaws with QURE that justify a bearish thesis either. Thus, I lean neutral on the stock, which is why I rate it a "Hold" for now, but it's a stock worth following in case AMT-130 for Huntington's disease provides a subsequent major update next year.

Caveats: Risk Analysis

In my view, the main risk with QURE is that most of its pipeline still remains relatively speculative. AMT-130 for Huntington's disease and AMT-162 for ALS are in Phase 1/2 trials, so I think it's still too early to take a confident, bullish, or bearish stance. This implies a long wait until QURE's pipeline IP develops into something more concrete. This is why I believe there can very well be better buying opportunities for QURE down the road, particularly since it's not cashflow positive despite already having an FDA-approved product. As it stands, I don't think QURE's royalty revenues from Hemgenix are sufficient to justify a much higher valuation.

On the other hand, in August 2024, QURE announced a restructuring plan to reduce its workforce by 65%, or approximately 300 jobs. This means that QURE is also focusing on extending its cash runway. Management believes they can reduce their cash burn by around $75 million. It's possible that these cost-cutting efforts, coupled with marginal revenue growth, could help QURE in its objective of reducing $50 million of its financial debt. If this materializes, it could help QURE transition into a more sustainable entity. Still, I believe we should wait until next quarter to see if this strategy is working as intended.

It's also worth mentioning that on July 23, the company sold its gene therapy manufacturing facility in Lexington, Massachusetts, to Genezen. In the process, QURE also acquired a substantial stake in this company. This transaction led to QURE's CEO joining Genezen's board, which could potentially improve their partnership's efficiency and manufacturing. However, overall, I believe there are too many uncertainties now with QURE. I feel it could go either way, especially after considering its highly speculative and early-stage IP. So, ultimately, I think it's prudent to rate QURE a "hold" for now, but it's worth monitoring how it evolves in its next Q3 report.

Watchlist for Now: Conclusion

Ultimately, QURE's main value drivers are its gene therapy platforms. I also think that AMT-191's recent FDA Orphan Drug designation is a promising green flag for its prospects. Similarly, AMT-130 for HD and AMT-260 for Temporal Lobe Epilepsy could eventually grow into valuable IPs. Yet, at the same time, it's difficult to ignore the ongoing yellow flags due to the early stages of its pipeline and the ongoing fundamental changes with its restructuring plan. Moreover, I don't see any major catalysts in the near term that can fundamentally improve QURE's prospects. Thus, it's prudent to lean neutral for now but keep an eye on it with its next quarterly report.

data.title: JD.com: Valuation And Fundamentals At An Inflection Point
data.description:
I last covered JD.com (NASDAQ:JD) (OTCPK:JDCMF) in my article JD.com: Why I'm buying for the long term, a year ago. Not only did I believe the company was a buy, but I also argued the case for why JD could achieve a 19% annualised return over the next 5 years. This strong return would be driven by revenue acceleration & margin expansion, driving strong EPS growth, coupled with a cheap starting valuation allowed for the opportunity to achieve excess returns.

I argued that the revenue growth would be driven by improvements in the macro environment, the lapping of self-inflicted actions, and market expansion from 3P & general merchandise revenue. Since the last article, revenue growth has remained slow, while margins have impressively continued to expand driven by operating leverage within its logistics segment.

In this article, I will provide an update on the company's progress towards accelerating revenue growth and argue why I still believe JD is a buy.

Valuation

In the last article, I argued JD was cheap on both a price/sales ratio and by using a DCF model. Despite the stock increasing 26% since I last published, I still consider the company to be cheap, with the market pricing the company at a level that implies the company will shrink, rather than grow as I expect it to.

When running a reverse DCF, these low expectations become evident. Using the following parameters:

Share price $40
EPS $2.72
Discount rate 12%
Book value per share $20.79

The market is implying that the company's earnings will shrink by 1% annually for the next 20 years. I believe this to be unlikely, in fact, it's likely that earnings will grow over this time period, creating a valuation gap. Using the same parameters above, JD is likely to be worth around $59 if the company can grow EPS at a conservative 6% for the next 10 years, creating almost 50% upside.

The company is even more undervalued when EPS is substituted for FCF/share. Using FCF would make the company worth $61 with 0 growth in the future. I believe this provides a wide margin of safety for investors with potentially significant upside.

Macro Environment

In my previous article, I wrote about the macro environment slowing JD's growth. A year on the macro environment continues to be weak in the short term; the prolonged slowdown has lasted longer than I expected, creating headwinds for revenue growth. The headwinds JD faces are likely greater than those of its E-commerce counterparts.

The economy continues to be affected by the nationwide housing downturn along with geopolitical tariffs imposed by the EU & US. This has caused consumer confidence to drop and remain weak ever since. I shared a chart in my article, Alibaba: fundamentals at an inflection point demonstrating the pronounced drop and subsequent weakness in the confidence of the Chinese consumer.

The weakness in consumer confidence and the downturn in the housing market is especially troublesome for JD because of its key presence in the home appliance and electronic markets. Big ticket items like these have seen a drop over 2022 & 2023 with the home appliance industry still below its 2021 highs. Property transactions primarily drive home appliance sales, when a consumer buys a new property this is the ideal time to buy new appliances or refresh existing ones if moving. Without these transactions, the industry is more reliant on consumers replacing existing appliances, creating a slowdown in the industry.

The consumer electronic market, another core area for JD, has also likely seen a greater impact than the overall market due to the big-ticket nature of the products. The low consumer confidence leads to the extension of the product life cycle as consumers choose to prioritise saving money rather than upgrading.

Since the end of September, The Chinese government has unveiled a host of measures to boost the struggling economy, including lowering the reserve ratio by 50bps, releasing $142.5B of liquidity, lowering existing mortgage rates, a $71B swap facility for brokers & funds to buy stock, adding additional liquidity. These measures, along with new local incentives to boost consumption, should help to improve consumer confidence, allowing JD and other E-commerce companies to enjoy the tailwinds from the long term growing middle class, increased consumption and increased E-Commerce penetration rates which currently stand at 30%. Although there is no guarantee these measures will work, China seems to be stepping up the aggression of the stimulus packages, giving me confidence that should this effort not be enough, further actions seem increasingly likely.

Market Share Gains & Home Appliance Stimulus

Along with broader stimulus measures, local authorities have also introduced incentives to replace old home appliances, offering 2,000 yuan per appliance. This incentive roughly equates to a 15% discount on home appliances. This stimulus could create a large product replacement cycle, given most home appliances are old, consume a lot of energy and are likely far worse than the latest models out there today given their age. JD is well-positioned to capture the growth from this stimulus for several reasons.

JD provides the best service by leveraging its integrated logistics services for fast and reliable delivery, the company also offers a one-stop trade-in service, which allows for free dismantling and pick up of old appliances and free delivery and installation of new appliances. This delivery capability, and service standard along with JD's price guarantees make it very difficult to compete with them in this area. This advantage is demonstrated by the consistent market share gains JD has been making over the past year. In the past year, first-time users of JD's trade-in service have increased by 200%, with only 20 cities/regions having introduced the stimulus. With over 300 cities having a population of over 1 million, JD will likely continue to experience growth from this initiative, helping the company grow and continue to gain share.

Despite growth appearing to slow in Q2 2024, it's important to note the tough comparison from the prior year period. I expect this growth to likely accelerate due to improving consumer confidence, an incentivised product refresh cycle and market share gains.

Operating Philosophy enhancements

JD has long held the philosophy of providing customers with products with speed, quality, and value. Over the past several years, JD has been making additional enhancements to those aspects of the business, creating financial headwinds for the business but, importantly, improving the user experience.

The actions JD has taken include the CNY 10B discount program, reduced free shipping threshold to CNY59, one-click price guarantees, instant refunds, free doorstep pickups for returns, cash back for delayed shipping along with the previously mentioned service enhancements for home appliances.

Most of these actions are targeted at improving product value to compete with competition which are also focused on providing improved value. This is creating headwinds for ASPs for direct sales, along with margin headwinds due to lower free shipping thresholds and lower product margins.

Other than an improving macro environment and long-term consumption tailwinds, I expect several factors to allow JD retail revenue to accelerate.

Improving usage trends

As a result of the continuous enhancements made to the retail product, JD has experienced positive user trends. Over the past year, JD has experienced double-digit quarterly user growth from both higher & lower-tier markets, demonstrating the broad-based attractiveness of JD's platform. JD has also experienced double-digit growth in user engagement, purchase frequency and order volume, demonstrating the success of JD's enhanced strategy; this success is increasing their NPS scores as the platform becomes a more attractive place to buy. These positive trends, however, are mostly offset by decreased order value because of the low-price strategy and lower free shipping thresholds.

Over time, I expect JD to lap the effect of their value strategy, allowing the revenue gap to close up to user trends.

3P

Despite being the nascent part of JD's retail platform, revenue growth from 3P sources such as commission and advertising has been slower than that of the direct sales part of the business. This slower growth is due to the company's focus on fostering the ecosystem and attracting merchants and users.

JD has been taking actions to increase the number of merchants on their platform, such as improving merchant tools, improving traffic allocation mechanisms, lowering platform service fees & take rates, in some places commission-free offers, simplifying the joining procedures & increasing merchant support, and lowering operating costs for sellers. These enhancements and the lower monetisation rates have created headwinds to the segment growth over time.

Beyond the financial impact, the merchant-friendly actions JD has taken have likely resulted in at least a doubling of merchants in the past year, with the number at over 1 million. This provides the platform with an increased selection of goods for consumers while improving the NPS score, creating a better experience. These actions are great for the long-term health of the platform while sacrificing some short-term growth. JD still has a long way to go to reach rivals Alibaba, in 2021 it was reported the number of merchants could be as high as 10 million. It's unlikely JD will ever reach that figure with such strict controls on quality and user experience; however, it's clear there is still room to attract new merchants.

Revenue in this segment is likely to accelerate for several reasons. First, the company is starting to lap the effects of reduced commission rates, this should allow the company to see the benefit of the additional merchants they have acquired over the past year. Second, the opportunity to increase service fees over time. With the news of Alibaba planning on increasing its service fee to 0.6%, this paves the way for competitors like JD to implement similar price increases over time should they wish. Finally, growth in advertising revenue, although advertising revenue has grown healthily in the double digits already, as JD attracts more merchants & users to the 3P side of the platform, there will be an increased demand to advertise. This increased demand should sustain a healthy growth rate over time.

The result of these growth drivers, and the capital-light nature required to generate these revenue streams, will not only benefit the top line but will allow for margin expansion on top.

Supermarket

JD supermarket is embedded in the general merchandise category; I expect it to be a driver of revenue over time. Begun in 2018, 7 Fresh is JD's supermarket brand. The company leverages JD logistics cold chain capabilities to sell fresh produce amounting to as much as 75% of sales, providing on-demand delivery or pickup while maintaining the quality and trust hallmarks JD has built. JD initially planned to open 1000 stores within 5 years. Store numbers haven't been reported in the last several years. However, it is unlikely JD has even reached 100 stores to date, with the last known store number of 47 in 2022. The slower-than-expected rollout is likely due to the impacts of the lockdown & recent strategic refocus within JD. During the lockdown period, the supermarkets experienced strong demand; this high base caused a temporary slowdown in 2023 as the company lapped a more normalised environment. Growth has resumed in 2024 with two-quarters of double-digit revenue growth; this growth is set to continue for the remainder of the year. In the long term, I expect the supermarket segment will drive growth for the business as the company works its way towards that 1000-store goal, more than 10x today's count.

Takeaway

Despite the revenue slowdown, margins have continued on their upward trajectory. I expect several factors to drive JD's revenue to accelerate over time, including an improving macro environment, market share gains in the company's core segments and increasing contributions from the 3P business & the supermarket segment.

JD's stock price is currently priced for no growth; I believe this creates the opportunity to buy the business at an attractive valuation while fundamentals remain weak.

data.title: Healthpeak: Potential Upside Still Exists, But Don't Expect Much
data.description:
Dear subscribers,

I wrote on Healthpeak (NYSE:DOC) about 3 months back when the company moved up above $20/share, and I started to see rates of return for my position of above 30% including FX and dividends. The company has moved up slightly further since, but a few days ago fell a few pp, which leads me to update here and call the company attractive, though perhaps "the last time" before it moves up again.

In my investments, I try to look for 15% annualized at a conservative multiple - that's the basis of my entire strategy, and so far, and for the last 9 years, it has worked out quite well.

In this article, I'll update Healthpeak and show you why I believe this to be the last time you can get 15% annualized at a conservative multiple. There has been a lot of doubt about investing in Healthpeak - especially at the trough below $17/share. The same was true for many of the Office REITs I invested in - which are now obviously higher at a very significant level. 100% forecastability is not a possibility, but I believe it's possible to with a high degree of accuracy spot undervalued quality.

Healthpeak was undervalued quality. Calling it massively undervalued quality here is wrong - but it's certainly somewhat undervalued.

Healthpeak - why it may be an investor's last chance for 15% annualized for some time for this company

With rates going down, it shouldn't be a surprise to anyone that REITs are getting more and more favored here. I've already acted on some positions I hold in some of the more overvalued REITs (as I believe them to be), in apartments and multifamily, among other things. Healthpeak is a company that's not close to "overvalued" here, but that's moving towards a position of full valuation.

And the reason for it is very clear to me.

Healthpeak Properties is a BBB+ rated business with a long-term debt/cap of less than 51%, a well-covered AFFO payout ratio of less than 85%, and a yield of 5.4%. My own YoC on Healthpeak, given my low average buy cost, is closer to 7%.

The company expects AFFO of at least $1.5 for the year, upwards of $1.56 possible. A very tight range, and completely in line with the estimates by consensus for this company, with a same-store growth of 2-3.7%. This is not massive, but this is also a very mature business.

The upside for 2025E that the company is already seeing is another $60M worth of cash NOI from some downtime at one of Its trophy lab campuses, and an additional $20M of merger synergies, which are already delivering above the targeted level. The company's updated 5.2x net debt/EBITDA makes this one of the lower-levered REITs in the segment, which is reflected by the BBB+ as well as the lending rate of 4.5% for a new, recent $750M term loan swap.

The new company merged with DOC (Formerly Physician's Realty Trust) has been closed now for 7 months, and while it's way too early to call everything here a "complete success", I do believe it's fair to call the progress so far encouraging. Management internalization of properties is finished in four markets, with an additional five completed in 2Q24, and the company has already realized $69M of proceeds from seller financing loans.

Structural demand drivers for this company are the same as for every company exposed to the aging population in the West - outpatient care trends, scientific/pharma discoveries, and in this case, a low exposure to the overall medical/SHOP field. You may notice that I cover very few nursing home/SHOP players with significant exposure to these fields - and the reason for this is that these players are more volatile and uncertain given the current political climate - so I'd rather own them extremely cheaply, or not own them at all at this time.

Merger benefits, as announced, are being realized.

For the last quarter, 2Q24, the company's FFO/AFFO saw growth of around 4.5%, and the company once again increased its diluted earnings guidance at the midpoint.

DOC also continues to optimize its portfolio. By this, I mean that the company is divesting assets, in this case, outpatient medical sales at 6.8% blended TTM Cash Cap rates during the quarter and of July, which brings total 2024A dispositions to $1.2B at an average of 6.5%. These proceeds have been used in part to buy back shares, a total of 10.5M at $188M in 2024 so far. New leases for the quarter were solid, with mark-to-market rent renewals of between 4.7-6%, with 180,000 sqft executed as of July.

Despite the dispositions in the outpatient medical field done by the company, the company still owns 507 properties in this segment. The company has focused on selling off older buildings with a relative average age of above 25 years, with low rental rates/percentages, and low percentages on campus, and with significantly lower population density rates and population growth rates in the areas involved - almost 100 bps less than in what remains.

I personally do not expect DOC to outperform in terms of growth for a few reasons. First, the company recently 2Q24 confirmed that the 3% escalators we're seeing in the MOB renewals, that's mostly where things are going to be. I was also in favor of the company's buyback moves - because DOC was trading at a considerable value discount to even conservative multiples or percentages of the real estate value. The company itself also acknowledges even as early as July that the profitability from buybacks here is lower - and today, it would be even lower. Management to me, in this REIT, are very capable capital allocators.

The company also continues to enjoy high rates of leasing and low rates of vacancy. All of the company's segments - from Outpatient care to lab business, the companies two segments, are seeing positive trends.

There are also positive trends for Biopharma and cap raising in the sector, pointing to further positive trends going forward.

Overall, Healthpeak has been doing very well. The company's trends continue to point in the right direction, reflected also by growth estimates. However, the low growth estimates of around 3-4.5% average per year here are what gives me cause for going in more conservatively than most analysts. Let me show you what I mean in the valuation segment.

Valuation for Healthpeak - Positive, but possibly the last opportunity of a conservative 15% annualized.

Healthpeak, at this point, trades at a conservative P/AFFO of 14.25x. This is no longer cheap, which it was early this year, but it would also be a stretch to call the company 'expensive' here. The reason is that at the 3-7 year average, the premium for the company has been increasing, moving from a 15-16x P/AFFO to somewhat of an 18-20x P/AFFO. If we forecast the company at 18-20x P/AFFO, then finding 15% annualized, or even rates above closing in on 20-23% annualized is not a problem.

Is such a development possible? Absolutely.

Is it likely? Given the company's estimated growth rates, which by the way derive from their own data, I do not think so. A 5.5% yield, while good, is nothing outstanding even at BBB+. There are equally conservative companies giving us similar rates of dividend, or returns, but with even better financials. My reasoning therefore to give the company a lower average multiple, and therefore a PT, is based on the assumption that the company's average valuation multiple will decline when the company's average growth normalizes.

Makes sense, right? If the company grows more, we can evaluate it at a higher rate. But lower, it doesn't make as much sense to allow a company 20-30x multiples when the company doesn't grow much. That's the whole basis of "growth versus income". And DOC is not a growth-oriented company. It grows slightly, but no more than slightly.

And these estimates we're seeing for DOC - they have a 100% forecast accuracy over a 10-year basis, even with a 10% margin of error. So the likelihood of them materializing exactly as such, I'd consider them pretty high.

So if we give the company between a 15-16x valuation, or the 20-year average of 16.2x, that means that we just barely have that 15% annualized that we're looking for.

And this is mostly due to the slight recent dip. We're moving between a 14.9%-15.3% annualized here, and if the company were to move firmly upward to $23-$24, it's my stance here that a conservative upside for the company would be "gone" and DOC would no longer be as investable.

You can see even in the graph above that such a valuation or such a price for DOC is nothing strange. DOC has been there before, in fact, only 1.5 years ago. To see the company moving up to 18.5x P/AFFO would not be strange - though at such valuations, I would start considering the rotation of the company's shares to more attractive opportunities. To me, it's all about what returns I can get from a company given the valuation we have. Some companies I would be willing to "let lie" for a longer time than others. Realty (O) and Agree (ADC) are good examples of such businesses. Given the relative historical volatility of this company, though, this is not something I'd be all that willing to do for a long time with DOC - because of the growth rates we're seeing here. I also don't believe we'll see another ZIRP, which is when the company moved up to 25-26x P/AFFO.

For that reason, I maintain my conservative PT of $27/share, which is my rotation target, and say that this is one of the last times you might get 15% annualized at a conservative price, and if you don't own DOC, then this is a reason to consider owning it.

Risks? Of course, some risks do exist - the prevalence of the company's SHOP portfolio, its concentration, and the number of lab campuses. The company is also exposed to risks in the outpatient medical sector. While the model is financially attractive, it comes with patient risks which at this point might not be as well-researched as we might want them to be. Medical offices and outpatient facilities often have patient acuities, staffing models, physical layouts, and internal policies different from other operations. The short of it is, that we'll need to know more about outpatient-localized risks, and companies like DOC are in it.

Still, it doesn't mean that I consider the company to be impacted or too much of a risk, and the thesis is the following.

Thesis

  • Healthpeak is one of the better healthcare REITs out there. Its portfolio is sound, its fundamentals are safe, the yield is extremely well-covered, and the company has an attractive future prospect based on both stability and slight growth of its prospects. The next few years will be tough for REITs in the space, but I believe that DOC (new Symbol) will be one of the REITs that survive and thrive. As of October 2024, this has proven to be a correct estimate - for the time being.

  • Based on this, I consider this company an attractive "BUY" at a good price, where we can see a conservative double-digit upside. Ever since selling off its senior portfolio, the company's earnings capacity has been declining from 2015 levels - but it's stabilizing, and I see a potential for some, if not massive growth in the next few years.

  • I give the company a conservative P/FFO of at least 15-17x, implying a long-term PT of $27/share, and an upside of at least 15% here. I maintain this as of this article, and this means the company is still a "Buy" for now.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.

  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.

  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.

  • This company is currently cheap.

  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversion.

This means that the company fulfills every single one of my criteria, except being cheap.

data.title: Attention Bulls; Rising Risk After Election Day, More Than Before
data.description:
Attention Bulls; Rising Risk The Day After Election Day, More Than Before.

I say attention bulls because at this point only a curmudgeon would see a reason to be a bear. Perhaps, I am the unreasonable curmudgeon, that will be for you to decide. Simple logic would dictate that there could be selling going into the election because truly no one has handicapped a presidential election correctly in a long time. This race, like the last few, is very close, so naturally there could be some nerves going into the poles. Mild selling going into the election is not what I am talking about, there could be a very large but short dislocation coming up.

What I have to say is not political

I don’t care who gets elected as president, I prefer that neither party sweeps. So as long as one party has the Senate and the other has the House which most pundits expect, I will be sanguine. In fact, I think that is why the markets have been at record highs. The big problem is the ballot counting for the presidency. The stock market hates uncertainty and not knowing the president for days, and certainly more than a week will be very difficult for the market. Looking at history the Senate will likely be decided quickly, but the House will not. The sheer number of house elections every year in small districts all over the country is likely to take as long as the presidency. However, even if it's a few days, I think that will be too much for the market to handle.

I am not predicting as much as I am pointing to a risk.

The stock market has shaken off concern, showing that it is a very able prognosticator in its own right. Markets have very strong predictive power, and that is why there are exchanges now that allow wagering on political outcomes. In fact, a recent last-minute ruling made wagering on the presidential election legal in the US, according to Barron's. If there was a reason to be concerned about whether we will know the outcome or not, we should start to see it reflected in the market, if not tomorrow, then in the latter part of the week.

How bad could it be?

I can’t imagine it being too bad, like I said, as long as the House and Senate aren’t a clean sweep, I think 4% to 5% of a drop, especially if the presidential election drags out for a week. I think the worst-case scenario is that the House is an unknown and the presidency is an unknown, then if it drags on, we could see 7% to 10% down. Look at this chart of the 6-month Nasdaq-100 ETF (QQQ).

If you were expecting me to point out something bad on this chart, forget it. I wish all my stocks looked like this. The Nasdaq composite and the Nasdaq-100 are lagging behind the S&P 500 in making new highs, but still, this is a beautiful chart. So, I am not going to point out that if the market fails here, it will form a double top, and that could bring in sellers. There is something about an uptrend that is almost 6 months old, everyone who wants to join the party already has. The few holdouts are not about to jump in now. I want to make a PSA to all of you who have double, maybe near triple-digit gains, please lower your leverage. I have not put out this warning in a long time, if you are trading on margin, and you are “all in with every penny” please do yourself a favor and just stop using margin, right not. Otherwise, you may regret it, if not this election cycle, then for some other reason, the market has an extended sell-off. The worst part is when the margin clerk sells you out at the bottom. Don’t be that guy or gal.

This also goes for those who are using options as well. If you are over-indexed with options trades, please back off here. Not because there is an imminent threat, but if you have been successful in trading these last several months, you might have grown your trading account larger than your investment account. Lower your risk and rebalance. Do it now while everyone is super-bullish. Maybe the November Presidential Election goes great, and we know who the president is that night, and nothing happens. At some point, you will see the downside of being over-levered, so just a friendly reminder here, sometimes you need an umbrella, or snow tires. You better have them ready before it rains or snows. I advocate using options instead of trading on margin, especially if you are new to trading. It takes a lot of discipline not to overdo margin leverage. The discount brokers make it so easy for you since there are no trading commissions now this is their biggest moneymaker. There are no warnings, no one telling you to back off before you lose a chunk of your portfolio.

Okay, what to do now?

Well, I am going to reduce my exposure in my trading account. You see, I am over-indexed on my trades right now. So this article like everything I write is about my assessment of my active investing and trading. I am too full of trades right now. Also, my investment account is doing great, like probably all of yours. I held on to the big chunk of cash that I allocated to my investments at this point. My philosophy is to never sell investments, so if the market does sell hard I have to mentally prepare myself for a hit to that account. So instead I am going to close out most of my trades this week and start thinking of hedging the day before the election. It’s going to take a lot of mental strength to cut my positions since things have been going so well. I told my investment group this week; “when it starts to be so easy to make money in trading, that is the time to get conservative.” The main thing to do is start trimming positions, and that is what I will do. Maybe I will start hedging right away and then slowly close out my trades. Either way, my goal will be 50% Cash or a combination of hedges and cash by this Friday.

My trades this week are heavy in consumer discretionary names

These are all long calls going out to December and January

Affirm (AFRM) is payments and credit via BNPL. I call this a consumer-discretionary name

Amazon (AMZN), they have AWS, which is the biggest cloud name, but they are Amazon, a consumer discretionary giant

Clover Health (CLOV) is a tiny healthcare name, health tech really. They got a 4-star rating from Medicare, while Humana (HUM) did not.

e.l.f. (ELF) Make-up outfit running circles around the fancier labels – Consumer discretionary

Alphabet (GOOGL) is part of nearly every consumer’s research for discretionary purchases

SoFi Tech (SOFI) is fintech, and they are breaking out into double digits. It’s already a small position, so I will probably keep this name and see what happens.

Uber Technologies (UBER) Consumer discretionary. The stock fell a bit after rumors that they will make an offer on Expedia (EXPE) were published in the FT. This is very preliminary, and I expect UBER to continue to rise.

Finally, a biotech name that I have been holding for a while; Viking Therapeutics (VKTX). They have a presentation at the Obesity Week conference and I expect some new data readouts on their GLP-1 pill. Obesity Week starts November 2, so I definitely will be holding on to this trade.

Good luck friends. I am going to reduce leverage. I certainly would not ever be on margin.

data.title: I'm Changing My Retiree Model Portfolio For 2025
data.description:
Introduction

Since I posted my model retiree portfolio back in January, I've had some time to watch and see how it performed. I've also been able to watch other assets, potential contenders, and decide how to make changes for next year.

I typically do an annual review of my models for changes, and now it's time for the "DIY Retiree." I put that in quotes because this is not Nana's portfolio, but for the kinds of investors that make up my reader base, self-managers interested in building portfolios using readily available ETFs and mutual funds, and who are okay with a certain level of complexity.

This model has performed as expected since I recommended it, and I am very happy to say that it was successful in its goal of outperforming the 60/40 model. Since my initial recommendation, the portfolio has returned 14%, outperforming the 60/40 portfolio by 0.6%, and saw a drawdown of 2.6%, compared to the 60/40's 3.6%.

However, I believe that changes can be made to further reduce the volatility of the model while preserving gains on-par with or better than a traditional 60/40 portfolio.

The Old Portfolio

Without further ado, here is the portfolio as I initially recommended:

Ticker Name Weight
BLNDX/REMIX Standpoint Multi-Asset Fund 40%
ACWV iShares MSCI Global Min Vol Factor ETF 10%
LQD iShares iBoxx $ IG Corporate Bond ETF 10%
SVOL Simplify Volatility Premium Income ETF 10%
FOF Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opp. Fund 10%
PCEF Invesco Closed-End Funds ETF 10%
CEFS Saba Closed-End Funds ETF 10%

The portfolio is split into several themes:

  • Core
    • Standpoint Multi-Asset Fund
  • Direct Beta
    • Global Min-Vol Stocks
    • Investment Grade Corp Bonds
    • Short Volatility Strategy
  • Funds-of-Funds
    • Provides high income
    • Access leverage via discounts

Standpoint Multi-Asset Fund, the Core

The Standpoint Multi-Asset Fund (BLNDX/REMIX), is a mainstay of the portfolio at a massive 40% allocation. It alone is the core, and is a position that has not changed from the old model to the new 2025 model.

BLNDX/REMIX is a fund that holds stocks, bonds, and manages a futures overlay to bring stability to the fund and access downside protection through diversification. This has worked out very well for them, most notably when they navigated 2022 much better than a traditional 60/40, which fell apart that year.

Its major drawing point as a core 40% position in this portfolio is that it rarely has a negative year. In the last four years, it's run positive, and is on track for a positive performance this year as well.

This fund provides a foundation for the portfolio built on a massive investible universe, which will provide the portfolio with some resiliency to economic woes as it is diversified globally and across stocks, bonds, currencies, and commodities.

Direct Beta Exposure to Beta — 30%

To "juice up" the portfolio and add in some more risk, three funds were added:

  • iShares MSCI Global Min Vol Factor ETF (ACWV)
  • iShares iBoxx $ IG Corporate Bond ETF (LQD)
  • Simplify Volatility Premium ETF (SVOL)

All held at 10% each, this represents a core 30% of the portfolio which is being changed for the future. The idea was that adding in factor investing, risk-loaded bonds, and a short volatility strategy (which acts very similarly to pure equity risk) would provide excess returns to the portfolio on top of the beta we got through Standpoint.

I wasn't happy with how these funds interacted with the rest of the funds, and they did not end up contributing as much as I had hoped to the portfolio.

ACWV outperformed and was very competent, but I do not believe I want the equity risk this brings for the model. Standpoint comes with enough beta that I don't think ACWV is needed. It is still a good fund and a recommendation for investors looking for global stock exposure. It can be a fantastic core holding.

LQD underperformed, contributing more risk than reward to the portfolio. It's out, and I am not going to replace it with another passive bond fund. With rates in flux, I prefer the active management of bonds through the CEF exposure and Standpoint.

SVOL also outperformed, and I was very impressed with how its hedge interacted with the VIX regime in August/September. However, the drawdown that it experienced was still more than I was comfortable knowing the intention of the portfolio, so I am going to sideline it for now. That being said, I am a large proponent of SVOL in an income portfolio and believe it can act as a core holding as an alternative strategy.

When I introduce the new portfolio, these three holdings will be combined into one substitute fund that should improve the portfolio's overall resilience to market volatility and drawdowns.

Closed-End Funds-of-Funds — 30%

The last three holdings are the fund-of-funds, or funds that only hold other funds, where the goal was to take 30% of the portfolio and disperse it across three different portfolios of funds, meaning that it essentially acted as a "catch-all" across the CEF universe. As shown above, these funds did well in the risk to reward department.

These funds are:

  • Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunity Fund (FOF)
  • Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF (PCEF)
  • Saba Closed-End Funds ETF (CEFS)

They contributed significant amounts of income to the portfolio, which is important for a retiree. The money that we can take in dividends, the easier RMDs become because cash can be taken out first, then shares sold or transferred if necessary.

The New Portfolio

So what changes am I making? Here's the new model:

Ticker Name Weight
BLNDX/REMIX StandPoint Multi-Asset Fund 40%
MBXAX/MBXIX Catalyst/Millburn Hedge Strategy Fund 30%
SPE Special Opportunities Fund 10%
PCEF Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF 10%
CEFS Saba Closed-End Funds ETF 10%

Note: if you are going to implement this, it is more likely that you will have access to investor class shares of the mutual funds listed, and should check for REMIX and MBXIX before BLNDX and MBXAX.

Looking back at a 3-year backtest with these two allocations side-by-side shows us incredibly, eerily, similar outcomes — however, the New Model outperformed dramatically in volatility, which is the key change I wanted to make.

The Catalyst/Millburn Hedge Strategy Fund (MBXIX) — 30%

I am going to replace the 30% beta funds (stocks, bonds, volatility) with the Catalyst/Millburn Hedge Strategy Fund, which uses quantitative and alternative strategies to make its returns. It's designed to be an alternative asset to a portfolio outside the equity and bond risks we are already taking in the Standpoint fund as the core. This other core will offer protection from equity draw-downs, but still provide returns from other risk premia. Here is how both holdings faired against global stocks YTD.

Similarly to the Standpoint fund, the Catalyst fund trades across a wide array of assets and strategies, spreading out its risk across global equities, bonds, swaps, currencies, and commodities.

Special Opportunities Fund (SPE) — 10%

I wasn't happy with FOF's low yield and high distribution (which meant a fair bit of capital recovery), despite its overall good performance risk to reward-wise, so I am swapping it out with the Special Opportunities Fund, which focuses on buying CEFs at a discount. I highly recommend taking a look at this recent article from my colleague, Nick Ackerman, about the fund.

It is far more in line with the other funds yield-wise, and I am hoping for a higher income output for 2025 than we had this year so far without as much return of capital.

How Does it Perform?

Against the old model, here's the YTD comparison of the old and new. This is backwards looking, but I am looking to see if my changes affect the pain points I've mentioned. It offers marginally better performance total return-wise, but what it does offer is more stability. The new model's max drawdown far outperformed.

This significantly shorter drawdown shows how much we have reduced the beta exposure by, as that drop was much more apparent in the higher-beta Old Model and 60/40 Portfolio.

Below is a comparison between the new, old, and 60/40 models in various risk and return metrics. I've highlighted several important comparisons, including how the standard deviation is up with the new model, despite its max drawdown and 60/50 correlation being lower (benchmark correlation) than the old model's. Its beta is down from .81 to .51, and its R2 down to 30% from 80%. I am very impressed with how the changes would've played out this year, and believe that the new model is better prepared to handle future volatility.

Suitability

While this portfolio is recommended to retirees, not all retirees are the same. This portfolio is designed for investors who want to see less market risk and positive returns at the expense of total return. There is a value to peace of mind in outperforming during bear markets that is worth a lot more than you may initially price it.

This portfolio is for a moderately conservative investor who wants to hold this instead of a traditional 60/40 portfolio. Aggressive investors may consider putting ACWV back into the portfolio at 10% and reducing MBXIX to 20%.

Conclusion

I have updated my Retiree Model Portfolio, the model I recommend for DIY investors like my reader base, who are interested in building better portfolios with modern tools, leveraging actively managed and diversified funds like the Standpoint Multi-Asset Fund and the Catalyst Hedge Strategy Fund.

The strategy of the portfolio is conservative growth core through broad diversification of assets and strategies with a satellite array of high-quality closed-end funds to provide a moderate yield. This model is aimed at preserving capital and putting it to work, ensuring as low of drawdowns as possible, while still accessing leverage and high-yield assets via CEFs.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: BP: Finally We See A Catalyst
data.description:
On October 7, Reuters reported that BP (NYSE:BP) (OTCPK:BPAQF) management has apparently abandoned its previous target to cut production by 25% vs 2019 through the end of the decade. Citing unnamed sources, the report noted that CEO Auchincloss, appointed late in 2023, was considering a full strategic "reset" in order to regain investor confidence and turn around BP's share price underperformance.

While welcome, we believe the very muted share price reaction to the announcement (~1% gain on Oct 7) reflects primarily the lack of more precise guidance. With management apparently present a more concrete plan at next year's strategy update, released in tandem with Q4/FY results in March, we view the shift in course as broadly supportive, yet likely not tipping the needle in BP's equity story for the near term.

Given the divergence in multiples between BP and its primary UK peer Shell following the latter's 2023 CMD, we believe that in the mid-term, this, however, could become a key catalyst for shares to move from "value-trap" to "value" territory. With the company's capital return remaining best-in-class at ~14% on a 12m forward basis (sector: ~9%) and shares trading at a ~15% FCF yield, we reiterate our Overweight rating.

Factoring in weaker previewed Q3 results, we update our price target to $42/sh, yet continue to believe that BP offers what is likely the best risk/reward skew among global oils with the 2025 strategy update as a key catalyst. Key risks remain largely in lower than projected oil & gas prices and/or a broader deterioration in macroeconomic conditions.

[Note: Supermajor peers include Exxon (XOM), Chevron (CVX), Shell (SHEL) and Total (TTE). Other majors include Eni (E), Equinor (EQNR), Cenovus (CVE), Suncor (SU) and Imperial (IMO).]

Key Thesis

Management to reportedly scrap ~2Mboed production target by 2030, now seeing slight growth driven by the Middle East. On October 7 and citing unnamed sources, Reuters reported that BP's senior management would abandon the, at least officially, previous target to cut oil & gas production 25% through 2030. Based on 2019 figures, this would have implied a decrease in output from currently around 2.3-2.4Mboed to just over 2.0Mboed or roughly 15% from now. Instead, BP will apparently aim to keep output at stable to slightly growing levels through the decade, with the report also noting management was currently evaluating several projects in the Middle East, particularly Iraq's Majnoon and Kirkuk fields and redevelopments in Kuwait.

We note that shares did not react particularly positive to the report, with the stock up just around 1% over the day, which, in our view, is likely due to the lack of any clear guidance. As noted in the report, management refrained from issuing any concrete guidance on production trajectory, pushing any definitive outlook back towards its next strategy update in early 2025. While the outlook therefore remains subject to many variables, we believe BP could be targeting around 1-2% annual production growth, driven by the aforementioned Middle East projects, bpx, and the Gulf of Mexico Paleogene. This would put 2030 output at approx. 2.5Mboed or around 10% above current levels.

While the sources cited also noted that unofficially the target had been scrapped some time ago, we see the announcement as welcome, yet likely unable to move the needle until a clear plan is presented to investors in early 2025.

With next year's investor day likely providing a more detailed production guidance, we see potential for multiples to catch up to primary peer Shell. Anticipating the strategy update to be used for 1) outlining and highlighting a shift back to oil & gas and 2) therefore raising mid-term guidance for the segment, we see BP essentially following the Shell playbook. Shortly after Sawan's rise to the CEO post, Shell held a capital markets day in June 2023 during which it succeeded in convincing investors the company was committed to oil & gas while being laser-focused on (shareholder) returns. We believe Auchincloss & his team will aim to provide investors with a similar message with the update likely being a crucial point in his tenure, having so far failed to stop BP's underperformance vs peers with relative multiples having derated further.

Should he be successful, however, we believe this could finally be the catalyst shares have needed and lacked for quite a while. Having traded at roughly similar multiples prior, we note that there has emerged a significant valuation gap between BP and Shell, with the 2023 CMD a clear starting point. While we continue to see Shell as a higher quality stock, both due to management's more established track record and its LNG & trading advantage, we believe there is strong potential for the current multiple gap to (at least partially) close.

Capital return remains best-in-class, offering ~14% in dividends and buybacks for a ~15% FCF yield. With shares having fallen further, BP now offers a ~14% distribution yield, including a ~5.7% dividend yield. While trading relatively even with Shell on free cash flow, driven by the latter's relatively lower capital spending, we note BP's distribution yield exceeds Shell's by around 3% with a significantly higher base dividend (5.7% vs 4.1%) and a larger contribution from buybacks. There has recently been some concerns about management's buyback pace of currently $1.75B per quarter, yet we believe those concerns are likely overstated, barring any substantial and prolonged oil price weakness (see here).

Relative to the entire sector of global integrated majors, we see BP's distribution yield at a 5 percentage point premium while also trading at a FCF yield ~6% higher than peers. We believe this combination still offers one of the most attractive risk/rewards, even when accounting for a higher degree of near/mid-term uncertainty until BP management's formal strategy update.

Valuation Update

Flowing through the expected weaker Q3 results as well as lower expected oil prices for 2024 and 2025, we lower our price target to $42 per US ADR, implying ~33% price upside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Pfizer: Activists Clouding A Promising Picture
data.description:
As 2024 comes to an end, Pfizer Inc. (NYSE:PFE) (NEOE:PFE:CA) was poised to return to growth. Oddly, activists are now swarming the biopharma for changes the company already appears to be implementing, clouding the story. My investment thesis remains ultra-Bullish on the stock due to the deep valuation and the large dividend yield, but a distracting activist battle could distract the management team.

Stay The Course

Pfizer recently reported Q2 non-covid sales grew by 14% YoY to reach $12.8 billion. Total sales beat analyst estimates, but revenues only grew 3% to reach $13.3 billion due to weak Covid sales.

The biopharma raised 2024 revenues estimates by $1.0 billion, yet activists are now pressuring Pfizer to make changes. The worst part is that former executives are in the middle of the pressure, with signs of ex-CEO Read and ex-CFO D'Amelio dropping out of the activist campaign due to pressure from the company.

Starboard Value LP has apparently invested $1.0 billion in Pfizer (not a large sum) and made the following accusations to the BoD regarding management pressuring the former executives to renege their activist push:

  • Costly litigation against them
  • Claw back prior compensation
  • Cancel unvested performance stock units

Either way, Pfizer shareholders aren't better off under this scenario. The activist is either distracting management and/or management is too focused on blocking activists than fixing the company.

Pfizer has a market cap of $165 billion, with Starboard only controlling a fraction of 1% of the company. The focus needs to return to finishing the plans already in place to streamline the business after the Covid drugs fizzled out.

The biggest mistake Pfizer likely made was chasing the Covid vaccine prospects too much, and the biggest mistake now is likely chasing another drug franchise, such as in weight loss. The biopharma had a disappointing setback in this sector, with danuglipron development being halted last year after unacceptable side effects. The lead by Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) would suggest a focus elsewhere is warranted, but Pfizer is still working on weight management drugs.

Moving Forward

Pfizer already implemented a cost realignment problem to remove $4 billion from the cost structure by this year, with another $1.5 billion targeted by 2027 via Phase 1 of the Manufacturing Optimization Program. The biopharma already bought Seagen to build the cancer business beyond Covid.

CEO Albert Bourla already appeared to be achieving results with the updated guidance after Q2. Seagen cost the company $43 billion and is expected to generate up to $5 billion of additional sales by 2026 with an original analyst target of hitting $9 billion by 2030.

The CEO needs to focus on driving sales for cancer therapies after the biopharma got distracted with Covid vaccines. Adcetris, Padcev, and Tukysa continue to progress well.

At the Morgan Stanley conference about a month ago, Pfizer CFO David Denton discussed the strong pipeline setup to surpass the large LOE losses towards the end of the decade:

So what we have is, I keep saying we have a little bit of an air pocket right now. Once we get to the back half of the decade, we have -- our launches of about 18 or 19 products are beginning to -- will begin to take hold more rapidly. We've made investments in Seagen as a good example and Biohaven and GBT, all of which have bigger growth aspirations in the '25 to '30 time frame.

And so, if you look at our LOEs during that time, we have, I know $17 billion roughly of LOEs in that timeframe. Our business development activity alone is, we modeled at $20 billion. So I think just the net of those two should imply a bit of growth in the back half of the decade. Then you couple on top of that just any performance coming out of our pipeline in our new product launches. And so I feel like the back half of the decade from a growth perspective, I can almost check the box.

Pfizer has already stripped out $4 billion in costs, placing the company back on a path to a $3 EPS generating $17 billion in annual profits. The company is spending around $11 billion on R&D to invest in the future, while acquiring several drugs in the past few years.

One huge negative about the investment story is the massive $70 billion of debt accrued due to the acquisitions like Seagen and the current $9.6 billion dividend payout. Pfizer does have $7.1 billion in cash and short-term investments.

Hopefully, management will listen to any ideas from shareholders, including Starboard, but the goal should be to execute on existing plans. Pfizer has the pipeline and cost cuts already in place to drive positive financial returns for shareholders.

The stock already pays a nearly 6% dividend yield while offering enough extra cash flows to pay down debt. The only real negative to the investment story here is a potential management fight with activist and too much of a push to chase a sector already dominated by large biopharma competitors.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Pfizer remains cheap at 10x forward earnings while offering a very strong dividend yield. If the company focuses on the existing cost realignment programs while investing in the current drug pipeline, shareholders should be awarded. If the company gets distracted with an activist battle and loses focus, shareholders could see limited benefits.

data.title: Texas Capital Bancshares: Preferred Shares Provide Attractive Yield
data.description:
Introduction

Texas Capital Bancshares (NASDAQ:TCBI) (OTCPK:TCBLL) is a regional bank with locations in Texas’ five largest metropolitan areas. The bank also offers a preferred share (NASDAQ:TCBIO), which I have covered a few times, most recently back in July when the bank’s preferred shares rallied to create an unattractive yield. Today, the preferred shares have not rallied commensurate with the market’s movements, and the yield on the bank’s preferred shares now represents an attractive entry point for income investors.

Texas Capital Earnings Report

Like all banks, Texas Capital is dealing with the challenges associated with rapidly higher interest rates, followed by those rates staying higher for longer than anyone could predict. In the case of Texas Capital, asset yields rose from under 2.5% to near 6%, but borrowing yields rose even more, going from 0.5% up to near 5%. The drop in net interest spread (asset yield less borrowing yield) placed pressure on the bank’s earnings, but in the last two quarters, borrowing yields have dropped, and net interest spreads have risen. Additionally, the bank has managed leverage well during this time as the net interest margin remains well above pandemic levels.

From an income and expense standpoint, Texas Capital Bancshares had a great third quarter. Interest income jumped, while interest expense rose marginally. Net interest income (interest income less interest expense) had been on a steady decline during the past six quarters but staged a stunning upside reversal in the third quarter, nearly matching the highs achieved in late 2022. Needless to say, the prospect of lower interest rates appears to be benefitting Texas Capital’s net interest income.

Loan and Deposit Growth

A large part of the reason behind Texas Capital Bancshares’ successful third quarter lies in the bank’s ability to grow its deposits. During the quarter, Texas Capital grew deposits by more than 8.5%, vastly outperforming its peers which saw an 1.2% deposit increase in the commercial banking sector. Additionally, while loan growth has been robust over the last year, loan balances remained relatively flat during the third quarter.

Because of the deposit growth and muted loan growth, the bank’s loan to deposit ratio dropped noticeably in the third quarter to 83%. A lower loan to deposit ratio indicates a decreased need for the bank to fund its loan growth through external borrowings. In fact, Texas Capital has applied much of its deposit growth towards paying down external debt, with around 25% of its short-term and long-term debt being paid down in the last quarter. These declines in debt have allowed the bank to improve its net interest income and consequently, its earnings.

Risks to Texas Capital Bancshares

Loan performance remains a primary concern for Texas Capital Bancshares. Investors should also be mindful that the company is geographically concentrated in Texas, where swings in the local economy could affect the bank. Texas Capital does most of its lending to commercial entities, although mortgage financing is the fastest growing loan segment with the bank.

While the bank is paring back its exposure to commercial real estate loans, Texas Capital still has $5.3 billion of its $22 billion loan portfolio tied up there. Additionally, nonaccrual loans are slowly beginning to climb, reaching $89 million from $63 million a year ago. While these levels are relatively tame, the bank’s allowance for credit losses is standing at just 1.2% of gross loans, which can create earnings headwinds if the bank decides they need to build their allowance further.

Conclusion

Texas Capital Bancshares showed a great improvement in net interest income in the third quarter, thanks to a build in deposits and pay down of external debt. The bank is facing some headwinds when it comes to loan performance, but the preferred shares have very little risk of being impaired. The recent price movement in the markets has brought the preferred share yield on Texas Capital’s preferred shares up to near the top quartile of all bank preferred shares. I believe this represents a good opportunity and entry point for income investors.

data.title: Proceed With Caution: Factors To Consider Before Buying Dutch Bros Stock
data.description:
Dutch Bros (NYSE: BROS) is a popular coffee and beverage store that is relatively early in its journey from a regional chain to a national one. You can think of Dutch Bros as an early-stage Dunkin' Donuts or Starbucks (SBUX). The concept has substantial room for expansion across the country, potentially leading to significant revenue growth. The company started in 1992 in Grants Pass, Oregon, and is amid an expansion from the west coast southeastward. The company's long-term forecast is for 4,000+ shops within 10 to 15 years. Management targets a mid-teen annual percentage growth rate in store openings over that period. By the end of the second quarter of 2024, Dutch Bros reached 912 shops.

The following map shows the company's footprint at the end of 2023.

Investing in restaurant concepts early in their regional to national expansion can be very lucrative. An example of how profitable regional-to-national restaurant stories can become is my recommendation of Wingstop (WING) On September 11, 2020. As I write this on October 18, 2024, that recommendation produced a total return of 201.11%, compared to the S&P 500's (SPX) rise of 73.67% over the same period. However, unlike my recent recommendation of Portillo's (PTLO), another regional-to-national restaurant concept, the market may have already priced in the Dutch Bros expansion over the next 10 to 15 years.

This article will discuss Dutch Bros shops' operation and business fundamentals. It will also review the company's valuation, risks, and why the stock is a Hold at its current valuation.

Dutch Bros shops

Although Dutch Bros is similar to Starbucks in that it's primarily a beverage company, it differs because Dutch Bros uses a drive-thru business model where it conducts 90% of its business in a drive-thru setting. The other 10% consists of sales through a walk-up window. It has no sit-down accommodations in its stores. The company's 2023 10-K states:

Our shops typically have a smaller footprint than other drive-thru formats - this compact footprint allows us greater flexibility to place locations on more parcels. We typically target lots that are at least 25,000 square feet to handle substantial car volume. Except for a handful of legacy "coffee houses", all our shops deploy either a single or double drive-thru window with multiple feeder lanes for traffic flow.

The company has perfected the process of processing orders in its drive-thru business. The following image shows how management has set up the typical drive-thru.

Customers enter in a double lane that merges into one. The company equips employees named runners with tablets to greet cars in both lanes and take orders. Drinks are prepared within the stores by Broistas, which is what the company calls its baristas. Once the Broista finishes the drinks, the runners take them to customers in their cars. Some customers receive their drinks directly through the drive-thru window. Once customers receive their order, they can use an escape lane to get out of line and leave the drive-thru.

Dutch Bros has also started rolling out mobile orders, reaching approximately 200 shops by the end of July. Management hopes to achieve mobile orders in most of its stores by the end of 2024 and for it to be a growth driver in 2025. Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Christine Barone said on the company's second quarter 2024 earnings call:

Many of our shops have double drive-thru setups that include an escape plane, which could be utilized for mobile order. After checking in, our mobile order customers can be directed to the right-hand lane, where a runner can bring their drink if it is ready before the customer reaches the window. The customer can then escape using that open lane to their right, eliminating a potential bottleneck at the window. We already use this operational tactic in many of our locations.

Besides people liking the store's custom-ordered drinks, customers enjoy the fast and friendly customer service.

Although Dutch Bros drinks can serve hot drinks, the company specializes in iced beverages. It serves seasonal drinks, specialty drinks, energy drinks, iced tea, lemonade, chai latte, hot chocolate, shakes, smoothies, and sparkling soda. The company allows customers to customize their drinks, and it even sells a few snacks to go with the drinks.

The company's growth strategy is to open stores in areas where its drinks would likely be in heavy demand. Dutch Bros's 2023 10-K states its growth strategy:

"Leverage strong unit-level economics, ample liquidity, and robust underwriting standards to open shops with attractive returns, balancing building types, lease financing structure, and market entry strategies as needed."

Company fundamentals

Dutch Bros grew company-operated stores by 29% and total stores by 21% year over year in the second quarter of 2024. Investors should expect the company to produce a year-over-year store expansion above 15% consistently. If new store growth drops below 15%, something may be wrong with its regional-to-national growth expansion strategy. CEO Barone said in the second quarter 2024 earnings release:

"We added 36 shops in the second quarter, marking the 12th consecutive quarter of 30 or more new shop openings."

Investors should also watch the company's expansion into new states. In the first quarter, it became a coast-to-coast brand when it opened three new stores in Orlando, Florida, and one in Daytona Beach. Also, pay attention to the company's reception by customers when entering new markets. CEO Barone said on the first quarter earnings call:

"Our first Florida shop, which are 3,000 miles away from our original start in Grants Pass, Oregon, have been met with enthusiastic customer demand, a testament to the strength of our brand."

Another vital metric is the company's same-store sales ("SSS"), which measures its ability to retain and attract new customers.
